topic: Sigma Octantis dimness — verify c5 critical/major patches landed
change_summary: c5 critical+major+minor patches applied per decider Step 1d: n→28.8 replaced with n(r)≈2.84 + dome SHA c8eaeb1b context; 4%-closer math fixed to 97%-of-way-from-Canopus-to-rim with explicit gap/disc_radius percentages and per-position n values; precession 6,000y replaced with 14,000 AD / ~12,000y; Alpha Cen ~1.5 replaced with ~2 L☉ combined with A+B breakdown. Verify all four landed cleanly and cross-WIN consistency restored with Part 1 / WIN-065.
our_argument_summary: Polaris is intrinsically luminous (F7Ib supergiant, ~2,400 L☉); Sigma Octantis is intrinsically dim (F0III/IV subgiant, ~40 L☉). The magnitude difference is fully explained by stellar classification. Gaia DR3 photometry of 1.8 billion stars shows no hemispheric magnitude asymmetry. Bright southern stars (Canopus, Achernar, Alpha Cen) have observed magnitudes consistent with their intrinsic luminosities — no aetheric extinction signature. Defender-pivot to a pole-region-only claim is preempted by three counters: dome mechanism is geometric and continuous, Canopus is already pole-adjacent at 79% rim distance, and the pole-only retreat is unfalsifiable. Precession will sweep the south celestial pole within ~10° of Canopus by ~14,000 AD, inverting the asymmetry — incompatible with a fixed-disc-rim mechanism.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Sigma Octantis dimness is not monitored in the dome automation suite — no monitor.py domain processes a Polaris/Sigma-Octantis brightness ratio. Dome's own OPEN-004 explicitly lists "Polaris invisible from southern hemisphere — cutoff formula not derived" as unsolved.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: N/A — no monitor.py entry for this WIN exists.
data_sources_used: Gaia DR3 (claimed) — but no fetch logic in dome repo
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Defender pivot from quantitative n(r) gradient to qualitative pole-only-asymmetry would render the WIN unfalsifiable; preempted in detail_verdict_text counter #3.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None at this cycle. The c5 critical patch corrected the n→28.8 stale value (from pre-0.20-coefficient era) and explicitly notes the dome's published formula at SHA c8eaeb1b uses 0.20.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No monitor.py domain for this WIN; dome OPEN-004 admits no derivation. Detail_verdict_text correctly states "no monitor.py entry exists for this value at all."
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard astronomy explains pole-star brightness asymmetry as a transient stellar-distribution coincidence (cf. Hipparcos catalogue and precession theory). Dome does not propose a competing computed value; OPEN-004 admits no derivation. So this is not a relabel — it is an unexplained observation labeled as confirmation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Hipparcos main catalogue (Polaris V=1.98, Sigma Oct V=5.42) was published 1997, widely indexed in Wikipedia and astronomy databases for decades. Dome incorporated as a WIN without monitor.py entry; trivial-transcription test fails per Part 3.1b.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: Per OPEN-004 the dome admits no derivation of the cutoff/dimness from disc geometry. The detail_verdict_text correctly captures the absence of a quantitative dome derivation.

c5_critical_n28_8_to_2_84.evidence: Current detail_verdict_text contains: "refractive index reaching n(r) ≈ 2.84 at the rim per the dome's current formula n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8537/H(r) − 1) (consistent with Section 1.8 — note: an older coefficient of 0.27 produces n≈28.8 at r=20,015 km, but the dome's published formula as of SHA c8eaeb1b uses 0.20)". Section 1.8 reference resolves to "1.8 The Coordinate Collapse" which uses n=2.84 at rim, n=1.44 at equator — fully consistent. The n→28.8 figure no longer appears as a current dome value; it is correctly contextualized as a historical-coefficient artifact.

c5_major_4_to_97_percent.evidence: Current detail_verdict_text contains: "Sigma Octantis at δ ≈ −89° sits at r ≈ 19,900 km, about 97% of the way from Canopus's position to the rim (a radial gap of ≈4,040 km, ≈20% of disc_radius). The dome's current formula gives n ≈ 2.06 at Canopus's position and n ≈ 2.82 at Sigma Octantis's — a 37% refractivity contrast across that gap." Math checks: 19,900−15,860 = 4,040; 4,040/20,015 = 20.18% (rounds to 20%); 4,040/(20,015−15,860) = 4040/4155 = 97.2% (matches 97%). The previously-stated "only about 4% closer" framing is gone.

c5_minor_precession_timescale.evidence: detail_evidence: "by approximately 14,000 AD (~12,000 years from now) the south celestial pole will sweep within ~10° of Canopus" — consistent with detail_extra "By approximately 14,000 AD the south will have a pole star brighter (V=−0.74) than Polaris is today". The earlier ~6,000-year figure is fully removed; no "6,000 years" string remains in the WIN-033 prose.

c5_minor_alpha_cen_luminosity.evidence: Current detail_verdict_text contains: "Alpha Centauri (δ = −60.8°, G2V+K1V, ~2 L☉ combined (A: ~1.52 L☉, B: ~0.5 L☉), 4.37 ly)". Specifies both the system total and the component breakdown — resolves the internal inconsistency.

cross_win_consistency.WIN-065 (Polaris systematic excess): Uses identical n(r) formula "n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8537/H(r) − 1)" and references Section 1.8 for the coordinate-collapse dilemma. CONSISTENT — both WINs now anchor the dome's n(r) on the same coefficient (0.20) and the same Section 1.8 framing.
cross_win_consistency.Section 1.8 (Coordinate Collapse): Part 1 prose says n=1.44 at equator (r=10,008 km) and n=2.84 at ice wall (r=20,015 km) using formula n(r)=1+0.20×(8537/H(r)−1). WIN-033 now matches: n≈2.84 at rim, n≈2.06 at Canopus position, n≈2.82 at Sigma Oct position. CONSISTENT.
cross_win_consistency.WIN-012 / Forensic-Zoom κ derivation (us: WIN-012 detail_claim explicitly cites "dome SHA c8eaeb1b" as the dome's current revision; WIN-033 c5-patched text also cites "as of SHA c8eaeb1b" for the 0.20 coefficient. CONSISTENT — same canonical dome revision is anchoring both n(r) and κ analyses.

holes_found.description: Terminology slip: "a 37% refractivity contrast across that gap" computes (2.82−2.06)/2.06 = 36.9%, which is the relative change in refractive index n, not refractivity proper. Strict physics terminology defines refractivity as (n−1); the relative refractivity change here is (1.82−1.06)/1.06 = 71.7%, not 37%. The absolute Δn ≈ 0.76 across the gap is the same number under either reading, so the rhetorical force is preserved — but a dome defender could nitpick that "37% refractivity contrast" is loosely worded.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace "a 37% refractivity contrast across that gap" with "a Δn ≈ 0.76 across that gap (37% relative increase in refractive index)" or "a 37% relative increase in n across that gap" for terminological precision. Optional, low priority.

holes_found.description: Mild tautology in defender-pivot counter #2: "no monotonic n(r) profile that gives n≈2.06 at r=15,860 km can leave Canopus entirely unaffected" reads slightly circularly — since Canopus IS located at r=15,860 km, saying "n=2.06 at that radius" is the same as "n=2.06 at Canopus", so "cannot leave Canopus unaffected" is restating the antecedent. The actual argument is sound (a moderate refractive index of 2.06 — well above water's 1.33 or glass's ~1.5 — should produce some extinction; observed Canopus magnitude shows none), but the phrasing buries it. Not a factual error; a clarity issue.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional rephrase: "An n value of 2.06 at Canopus's position should produce a detectable extinction/dispersion signature; Canopus's photometry shows none, falsifying the dome's monotonic radial n(r) mechanism on the equatorial-to-Canopus segment." Low priority — the current text gets there in adjacent prose ("Canopus's observed magnitude (V=−0.74) is fully consistent with its intrinsic luminosity and parallax distance, with no residual extinction term").

kernel_of_truth.description: The southern celestial pole region today genuinely lacks a Polaris-class bright star within 1° of the pole — Sigma Octantis at V=5.42 is at naked-eye threshold while Polaris at V=1.98 is the 48th-brightest star in the sky (data/wins.json:033 detail_evidence; carries forward from c5). The asymmetry is real, observable, and a long-standing rhetorical surface for flat-earth/dome cosmologies. The c5 critical patch did NOT change the kernel — it just corrected the numerical apparatus invoked to attack it.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Two facts unrelated to disc-edge aetheric depth explain the asymmetry. (1) Polaris is F7Ib supergiant ~2,400 L☉ at 433 ly; Sigma Octantis is F0 subgiant ~40 L☉ at 294 ly (data/wins.json:033 detail_evidence) — magnitude difference is fully predicted by Hipparcos spectral classifications. (2) The asymmetry is transient: precession (~25,772-year cycle) sweeps the south celestial pole within ~10° of Canopus by ~14,000 AD (data/wins.json:033 detail_extra; cross-references IAU SOFA precession routines). A fixed-disc-rim mechanism cannot produce an effect that inverts on precessional timescales. The dome's own OPEN-004 admits no derivation (raw-text/05-model.txt). The c5-patched defender-pivot now correctly anchors counter #1 in the dome's actual published formula (n(r)=1+0.20×(8537/H(r)−1), n≈2.84 at rim per Section 1.8) — making the argument internally consistent with Part 1 instead of contradicting it.

deeper_analysis: All four c5 patches (1 critical, 1 major, 2 minor) landed cleanly. The critical regression (n→28.8 in EXP-299 defender-pivot, contradicting Part 1) is resolved with the correct value (n(r)≈2.84) plus an explicit historical note about the 0.27→0.20 coefficient change anchored to dome SHA c8eaeb1b. Cross-WIN consistency with WIN-065 and Section 1.8 is restored — all three now reference the same formula with the same coefficient. The math in the patched paragraph is internally consistent: r-positions, gap sizes, and per-position n values all reconcile under the dome's azimuthal-equidistant projection and current n(r) formula. The defender-pivot argument now has slightly less rhetorical punch than the broken n=28.8 version (which implied a huge gradient from n≈2 to n≈28 across the southern hemisphere) — but the corrected argument is FACTUALLY DEFENSIBLE, which the broken one was not. Net: c5 review correctly identified the regression and the decider's patches restored integrity. Two minor terminology/clarity nits remain but neither is a factual hole. Verdict (Refuted by Data) holds with high confidence — the post-hoc test failure, the OPEN-004 derivation-admission, the precession-inversion argument, and the Gaia-DR3 photometric-uniformity finding are all independent of the defender-pivot paragraph and remain unaffected.

OPEN-004 reference (raw-text/05-model.txt) — claimed text not fetched this cycle; relied on c5 review attestation.
